title: Natural deodorant spray
subtitle: A witch hazel and arrowroot spray deodorant with tea tree and lavender
excerpt: Witch hazel provides mild astringency and carries the active ingredients; arrowroot powder absorbs excess moisture; tea tree and lavender tackle odour-causing bacteria. This is a deodorant, not an antiperspirant -- it neutralises odour without blocking sweat glands.
sourceNotes: Witch hazel astringent and antimicrobial properties: Elias et al., British Journal of Dermatology (2009). Arrowroot powder as a moisture absorber: cosmetic formulation reference. Tea tree antimicrobial activity: Carson et al., Clinical Microbiology Reviews (2006).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Keep each bottle to 100 ml for correct concentration.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make in small batches; shake before use as the arrowroot settles.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use within two months. The arrowroot settles at the bottom -- shake thoroughly before each use. Discard if the spray develops an unusual smell or if the arrowroot will not re-suspend on shaking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Steam-distilled extract of Hamamelis virginiana bark and leaves, usually with 14% alcohol. Mildly astringent, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory -- a standard base for toners and aftershave preparations.

content.text: as the base: its mild alcohol content gives it partial preservative properties and its astringency helps reduce surface bacteria. Arrowroot powder absorbs sweat; tea tree and lavender tackle the odour-causing bacteria that cause underarm smell. This spray needs a thorough shake before each use as the arrowroot settles.

items.prepNote: from a pharmacy; Hamamelis virginiana distillate

content.text: Measure the arrowroot powder into the spray bottle first. Add the witch hazel and swirl to combine -- the arrowroot will suspend temporarily. Add the essential oils. Cap and shake well. The arrowroot will settle between uses; shake for 10 seconds before each application.

Shake vigorously. Hold the bottle 5 to 8 cm from clean, dry underarm skin and spray two to three times. Allow to dry fully before dressing -- the arrowroot can transfer to fabric if applied to damp skin. Apply morning and reapply if needed during the day. The spray works best on freshly washed skin.

This is a deodorant, not an antiperspirant. It neutralises odour without blocking sweat glands. Most people switching from aluminium-based antiperspirants experience a 2 to 4 week adjustment period during which sweat output normalises. Effectiveness varies between individuals. If irritation occurs, reduce or omit the tea tree oil.
Keep at room temperature. Shake before each use. Use within two months. Discard if the spray develops an unusual smell or if the arrowroot clumps and will not re-suspend.
